description: Unified answer key for Euclid Book I (Propositions I.1–I.48) across three formal systems: System E (Avigad, Dean, Mumma 2009), System H (Hilbert, Grundlagen der Geometrie 1899), and System T (Tarski, 1959). All formulas use each system's native syntax. Proofs include correct justifications and line refs verified against the unified checker.

notation.ab: segment from point a to point b
notation.∠abc: angle at vertex b with rays ba, bc
notation.between(a,b,c): b is strictly between a and c
notation.same-side(a,b,L): a and b on the same side of line L
notation.center(a, α): a is the center of circle α

step_kinds.let-line: Construct line through two distinct points (§3.3)
step_kinds.let-circle: Construct circle with given center through point (§3.3)
step_kinds.let-point-on-line: Introduce point on a given line (§3.3)
step_kinds.let-point-on-circle: Introduce point on a given circle (§3.3)
step_kinds.CN1 — Transitivity: Equals to same thing are equal (§3.5)
step_kinds.CN2 — Addition: Equals added to equals remain equal (§3.5)
step_kinds.CN3 — Subtraction: Equals subtracted from equals remain equal (§3.5)
step_kinds.CN5 — Whole > Part: The whole is greater than the part (§3.5)
step_kinds.< trichotomy: Exactly one of a<b, a=b, b<a (§3.5)
step_kinds.⊥-elim: Discharge assumption by ⊥ — conclude ¬φ from Assume φ (Fitch)

H.name: System H
H.reference: Hilbert, Grundlagen der Geometrie (1899); GeoCoq hilbert_axioms.v

notation.IncidL(a, l): point a lies on line l
notation.BetH(a, b, c): b is strictly between a and c
notation.CongH(a, b, c, d): segment ab is congruent to segment cd
notation.CongaH(a, b, c, d, e, f): angle abc is congruent to angle def
notation.outH(a, b, c): c is on ray ab (a ≠ b, a ≠ c, b on segment ac or c beyond b)
notation.SameSideH(a, b, l): a and b are on the same side of line l
notation.Cut(l, a, b): line l separates a and b
notation.Para(l, m): lines l and m are parallel
notation.Disjoint(a, b, c, d): segments ab and cd share no interior point

Incidence.description: Two distinct points determine a unique line.
Every line contains at least two distinct points.

Order.description: Of three collinear points, exactly one is between.
A line cutting one side of a triangle cuts another side.

Congruence.description: Segment transfer: on a ray from A, lay off a segment equal to CD.
Segment addition: if BetH(a,b,c) and BetH(a',b',c') and CongH(a,b,a',b') and CongH(b,c,b',c'), then CongH(a,c,a',c').
Angle transfer: in a half-plane, lay off an angle equal to a given angle.

Parallel.description: Playfair: through a point not on a line, at most one parallel.

T.name: System T

notation.Cong(a,b,c,d): segment ab congruent to segment cd
notation.B(a,b,c): b is between a and c (or a=b or b=c)

axiom_schemas.name: A5 — Five-Segment
axiom_schemas.formula: a≠b ∧ B(a,b,c) ∧ B(a',b',c') ∧ Cong(a,b,a',b') ∧ Cong(b,c,b',c') ∧ Cong(a,d,a',d') ∧ Cong(b,d,b',d') → Cong(c,d,c',d')

axiom_schemas.name: A8 — Upper Dimension
axiom_schemas.formula: Cong(a,p,a,q) ∧ Cong(b,p,b,q) ∧ Cong(c,p,c,q) → B(a,b,c) ∨ B(b,c,a) ∨ B(c,a,b)

axiom_schemas.name: A9 — Euclid (parallel postulate form)
axiom_schemas.formula: B(a,d,t) ∧ B(b,d,c) ∧ a≠d → ∃x,y. B(a,b,x) ∧ B(a,c,y) ∧ B(x,t,y)

T.proposition_library_status: Not yet implemented. Propositions to be derived via the Tarski-to-Euclid bridge (GeoCoq tarski_to_euclid.v).

Prop.I.1.statement: On a given finite straight line, construct an equilateral triangle.

system_T.note: No T-system theorem library yet; sequent to be derived via Tarski-to-Euclid bridge.

verified_proof.goal: ab = ac, ab = bc, ¬(c = a), ¬(c = b)

on(g, γ), on(g, M), between(g, b, d)
on(f, δ), on(f, N), between(f, a, d)

conclusions: CongaH(a, b, c, d, e, f)

conclusions: CongaH(b, a, e, c, a, e)

Prop.I.14.statement: If with any straight line, and at a point on it, two straight lines not lying on the same side make the adjacent angles equal to two right angles, the two lines are in a straight line with one another.

Prop.I.16.statement: In any triangle, if one of the sides is produced, the exterior angle is greater than either of the interior and opposite angles.

Prop.I.17.statement: In any triangle two angles taken together in any manner are less than two right angles.

Prop.I.18.statement: In any triangle the greater side subtends the greater angle.

Prop.I.20.statement: In any triangle two sides taken together in any manner are greater than the remaining one.

Prop.I.21.statement: If on one of the sides of a triangle, from its extremities, there be constructed two straight lines meeting within the triangle, the straight lines so constructed will be less than the remaining two sides but will contain a greater angle.

Prop.I.24.statement: If two triangles have two sides equal respectively but the included angle of the first greater than that of the second, the base of the first is also greater than the base of the second.

Prop.I.26.statement: If two triangles have the two angles equal to two angles respectively, and one side equal to one side, namely either the side adjoining the equal angles, or that subtending one of the equal angles, they will also have the remaining sides equal and the remaining angle equal.

Prop.I.28.statement: If a straight line falling on two straight lines makes the exterior angle equal to the interior and opposite angle on the same side, or the interior angles on the same side equal to two right angles, the straight lines will be parallel.

Prop.I.29.statement: A straight line falling on parallel straight lines makes the alternate angles equal to one another, the exterior angle equal to the interior and opposite angle, and the interior angles on the same side equal to two right angles.

Prop.I.47.statement: In right-angled triangles the square on the side subtending the right angle is equal to the squares on the sides containing the right angle.

Prop.I.48.statement: If in a triangle the square on one of the sides be equal to the squares on the remaining two sides of the triangle, the angle contained by the remaining two sides of the triangle is right.

positive_tests.name: test_Prop_I_1_equilateral_triangle
positive_tests.description: Construct an equilateral triangle on segment ab.
positive_tests.goal: ab = ac, ab = bc, ¬(c = a), ¬(c = b)

positive_tests.name: test_SAS_via_Prop_I_4
positive_tests.description: Side-Angle-Side congruence via Prop.I.4: ab=de, ac=df, ∠bac=∠edf ⇒ bc=ef, ∠abc=∠def, ∠bca=∠efd, △abc=△def.

positive_tests.name: test_let_line_construction
positive_tests.description: Construct a line through two distinct points.

positive_tests.name: test_let_circle_construction
positive_tests.description: Construct a circle with center a through point b.

negative_tests.name: reject_mismatched_construction_text
negative_tests.description: Step text must match the construction rule conclusion pattern.

negative_tests.name: reject_canvas_as_justification
negative_tests.description: Canvas observations cannot serve as justification.

negative_tests.name: reject_unjustified_assertion
negative_tests.description: A step with no prior support is rejected.

implementation_checks: Construction steps must match the rule conclusion pattern AND satisfy all prerequisites.
Prerequisites are checked against the full known-facts set (premises + all prior accepted steps).
Named axiom justifications (e.g. Generality 3) route through the diagrammatic consequence engine.
Multi-conclusion axiom clauses cannot be autofilled; the user must type the statement manually.
Canvas observations cannot serve as justification; all proof steps must be symbolically justified.
Theorem application (Prop.I.x) requires all hypotheses to be met in the known-facts set.
System H proofs use IncidL/BetH/CongH/CongaH predicates; System E proofs use on/between/center/inside predicates.
System T proofs use only Cong and B predicates on points (single-sorted).
